title: Electrodeposition of brushite coatings and their transformation to hydroxyapatite in aqueous solutions
abstract:
Electrodeposition of brushite coatings and their
transformation to hydroxyapatite in aqueous solutions
Mukesh Kumar, Harani Dasarathy,* Clyde Riley
Materials Science Program, Department of Chemistry, University of Alabama in Huntsville, Alabama 35899
Received 20 May 1998; accepted 19 November 1998
Abstract:
In this study we examine the potential for using
brushite (dicalcium phosphate dihydrate) as a coating for
orthopedic implants. It was found that brushite is trans-
formed into a more stable calcium phosphate (hydroxyapa-
tite), regardless of the aqueous transforming media utilized.
The transformation was carried out at room temperature in
deionized water, deionized water with added calcium ions,
and modified Hank’s type solution without calcium and
magnesium ions, modified Hank’s type solution with cal-
cium and magnesium ions, and modified Hank’s type solu-
tion with added calcium ions. In several of the transforma-
tion systems it was noted that brushite provided an in-
creased amount of calcium ions to the transforming media
through solubility. The process was monitored using X-ray
diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and dissolu-
tion studies. Scanning electron microscopy and energy dis-
persive spectroscopy were also utilized for characterization.
The brushite coatings can be easily achieved electrolytically
on conducting substrates. The process is non line of sight,
simple, and inexpensive; and when using an electrolyte that
can sustain currents of ∼100 mA/cm2, the necessary coating
can be completed in about 3 min. © 1999 John Wiley & Sons,
Inc. J Biomed Mater Res, 45, 302–310, 1999.
Key words: brushite; hydroxyapatite; transformation and
dissolution studies of calcium phosphates
INTRODUCTION
Numerous criteria have been adopted for the accep-
tance of materials for orthopedic and dental implants.
Included are inertness to body fluids, rigidity, and
bone fixation. Most orthopedic biomaterials in current
use are not engineered to produce desired interfacial
biological reactions. They appear to be partially bio-
compatible and are in use because they seem to work
even though they show “mild foreign body reac-
tions.”1 In spite of this, titanium and its alloys and
alloys of cobalt and chromium are often the materials
of choice. In an attempt to engineer these metals and
alloys, they are often coated with osteointegrating
and/or osteoconductive materials such as calcium
phosphate ceramics (CPC). This enhances their bio-
compatibility, and it has been demonstrated that there
is an improvement in their metal to bone fixation
when they serve as implants.2–5 Ducheyne et al. pre-
sented evidence that indicates the rate and strength of
fixation of an implant to the host seems to be related to
the availability of Ca2+ and PO4
3−at the implant in-
terface.6–9
One possibility for achieving an engineered bioma-
terial surface that could supply increased amounts of
Ca+2 and PO4
+3 ions in the short term and smaller
amounts in the long term is a multiple coating of CPC
consisting of relatively soluble and insoluble phases.
This could be achieved by covering the metal implant
with crystalline hydroxyapatite [Ca5(PO4)3OH; HA],
which is relatively insoluble, followed by a layer of a
more soluble CPC phase. Another possibility would
be to apply a CPC coating that could supply increased
amounts of Ca+2 and PO4
+3 ions early in the implant
life and transform to a less soluble biocompatible CPC
with time. One such soluble phase that holds promise
for either possibility is brushite (CaHPO4 ? 2H2O, di-
calcium phosphate dihydrate, DCPD), which may be
applied quickly and cheaply over an incomplete layer
of crystalline HA or directly to the metal substrate. An
electrolytic method for coating brushite on metallic
substrates has been reported.10,11 Here we present a
modified procedure for producing brushite coatings
on titanium substrates and demonstrate that the coat-
ing can dissolve and subsequently reprecipitate as HA
in various media at room or body temperature.
*Present address: Hexcel Corporation, 3300 Mallard Fox
Dr., P.O. Box 2247, Decatur, AL 35601
Correspondence to: Prof. Clyde Riley
Contract grant sponsor: NSF EPSCoR of Alabama
Contract grant sponsor: NIH
Contract grant sponsor: NASA
© 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
CCC 0021-9304/99/040302-09
 MATERIALS AND METHODS
Electrolytes for making brushite deposits were prepared
by adding different amounts of calcium dihydrogen phos-
phate [also called monocalcium phosphate, Ca(H2PO4)2, Al-
drich Chemical Co., Inc.] to 100 mL of 1.0M KCl solution.
These mixtures were stirred at room temperature for 8 h and
then filtered through Whatman 540 filter paper (particle re-
tention >8 mm) to obtain clear solutions. The final pH of
these solutions was approximately 2.8. The compositions of
the various electrolytes are given in Table I. The second
column lists the amount of Ca(H2PO4)2 added to 100 mL of
1M KCl solution. In contrast, Redepenning’s electrolytic so-
lution was a saturated aqueous solution of Ca(H2PO4)2, re-
portedly at a pH of 3.5, with no other conducting electro-
lyte.10,11 The concentration of Ca+2 ions in the present study
is therefore much higher. Also, the presence of the conduct-
ing salt in solution allows this electrolyte to sustain much
higher currents.
Deposits were made on 1-cm2 titanium substrates, which
were roughened on 320 SiC grit paper (≈40-mm mean diam-
eter) and then grit blasted with the same size SiC powder.
These coupons were then ultrasonicated in acetone to re-
move any residual grease and oil. Care was taken to ensure
that acetone did not dry on the surface of the coupon by
washing it in deionized water. The prepared substrates were
stored under deionized water until use. These titanium cou-
pons served as the cathode in a two electrode electrochemi-
cal cell. Platinum foil, about 4 cm2 in area, was used as the
anode. To avoid edge effects, the nonplating sides of the
cathode were covered by insertion in a flat circular disk of
Pyrex glass such that only an unedged circular plating area
was exposed to the solution. The electrodes were positioned
1 cm apart facing each other in a vertical orientation. Depo-
sition was carried out for different times except for dissolu-
tion and reprecipitation studies where coatings were made
for 3 min. For all deposition times the current density was
galvanostatically maintained at 100 mA/cm2.
After the samples were coated with brushite they were
removed from the electrolytic solution, washed in slow
flowing deionized water, and surface dried in nitrogen. To
ensure that the deposited layer did not crack while drying,
it was necessary to achieve slow and complete drying of the
ceramic layer. This was done by slow drying of the depos-
ited samples in a large enclosed area for at least 24 h.
Dissolution and reprecipitation studies of these deposits
were conducted in various aqueous media. The composi-
tions of the transforming fluids used in this study are pre-
sented in Table II. Dry brushite coated samples made from
solution E (Table I) were used in the dissolution studies. The
samples were placed in flasks containing 50 mL of the vari-
ous fluids for periods ranging from 1 to 48 h.
The bond strength of the deposit was obtained with a
Sebastian Five pull tester (Quad Group, Spokane, WA).
Tests were made on the prepared titanium disks that were
coated using solutions A, B, C, D, and E (Table I). Because
the adhesion strength between the metal and CPC deposit
was of prime interest, deposits were also made for shorter
time intervals of 15, 30, 45 and 60 s. This was done to char-
acterize the change in adhesion strength with increased
thickness of the brushite coating. For this study, solution C
(Table I) was used.
Phase changes in the transformation of brushite to HA
were followed by X-ray diffraction. All samples were ex-
posed to X rays generated from a copper source operating at
30 kV and 50 mA (Rigaku, Geigerflex). Data were collected
at 0.01° intervals with a 3-s data collection time for each
angular increment. To reduce background signal from the
titanium, an angular incidence of 5° to the sample surface
was used. The brushite microstructure was examined with a
Jeol 820 scanning electron microscope (SEM). Because brush-
ite coated samples tend to charge and distort the SEM image,
samples were either viewed at low accelerating voltages (5–
10 kV) or gold coated (Hummer 6.0) prior to examination
under the microscope. Composition and elemental distribu-
tion were determined using a liquid nitrogen cooled Kevex
beryllium detector for energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS)
working in conjunction with the SEM. To determine the car-
bonate incorporation in the CPC during transformation, a
Perkin Elmer PHI 5400 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer
with a magnesium source was used. Atomic adsorption was
conducted using a Varian Spectra 40 with an electrodeless
discharge lamp to determine calcium ion concentration in
the various transforming media as a function of time.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
The amount of calcium dihydrogen phosphate dis-
solved in 1M KCl solution varied with the amount of
the material initially added to the potassium chloride
solution. The plot in Figure 1 shows the amount of
phosphate that actually dissolved in 100 mL of 1M
KCl solution. It is interesting to note that the plot is
almost linear, indicating that the ratio of the dissolved
amount to the total amount added remains constant.
The solubility of Ca(H2PO4)2 ? H2O in 100 mL of cold
water is about 1.8 g at 30°C.12 One explanation for this
observed phenomenon is the possible presence of an
extremely soluble impurity in the as-received calcium
dihydrogen phosphate. Another possibility is the
presence of KCl, which alters the apparent solubility
of the calcium phosphate. This may be explained in
terms of the activity coefficients of Ca+2 and H2PO4
−
ions in the presence of potassium ions. The activity

BRUSHITE COATINGS AND TRANSFORMATION TO HA

Different Electrolyte Compositions and Properties of
BRUSHITE COATINGS AND TRANSFORMATION TO HA
coefficient of Ca+2 in a solution of ionic strength of 0.5
is about 0.4 and decreases as the ionic strength in-
creases.13 The electrolytic solution used for brushite
deposition has ionic strength greater than 1; thus, it is
expected that the activity coefficient for the Ca+2 ion is
less than 0.4. If the activity coefficient of H2PO4
shows a similar trend, then the increased solubility is
easily justified on the basis of apparent solubility
−2)2]. As noted previously, the
pH of this solution is about 2.8. At this pH it is seen
from the distribution diagram for the phosphate fam-
activities are nearly equal.14 But at a pH of 3.5 there
is essentially (H2PO4)−in solution, thus making the
present bath considerably different from the electro-
The mass difference between the brushite coated Ti
coupon and uncoated Ti coupon was used to deter-
mine the mass of deposited brushite. The mass of cal-
cium dihydrogen phosphate added to 100 mL of 1M
KCl solution to make the electrolytic solution versus
the amount of brushite deposited after 180 s at 100
mA/cm2 is given in Table I. In column 3 the average
weights of brushite deposited have been tabulated.
These values were obtained by averaging over five
different deposits under the same conditions. The
mass of brushite deposited increases as the amount of
Ca(H2PO4) increases in solution, with the exception of
deposits made from solution D. These masses were
averaged over five determinations. It was observed
that sometimes during the deposition process deposits
can flake off because of the evolution of the hydrogen
gas. This may be the reason for the smaller mass of
brushite deposited from solution D.
The X-ray diffraction spectra of deposits with in-
creasing deposition time are shown in Figure 2.
Samples deposited for 30 s had a thin coating and
show X-ray diffraction peaks corresponding to both
brushite and titanium. In this figure a standard file for
synthetic brushite is shown as lines for comparison
from the Joint Committee for Powder Diffraction. In

X-ray diffraction spectra of brushite deposit as a
function of deposition time. (*) Titanium lines.
the spectrum corresponding to 30 s of deposition, Ti
lines are marked (JCPDS 5-682). Spectra from samples
that were deposited for 30 s match extremely well
with the standard, file (JCPDS 9-77). However, for
longer deposition times it is noted that, even though
the peak positions match, there is mismatch in peak
intensities. This implies that the deposits tend to crys-
tallize such that certain crystallographic planes are ex-
posed to the solution. A typical spectrum of the as-
deposited ceramic, deposited for 3 min, from solutions
C, D, and E (7, 8, and 10 g calcium dihydrogen phos-
phate in 100 mL of 1M KCl solution, respectively) is
shown in Figure 2. The standard places the major
peaks at 11.7, 20.9, and 29.25°. The relative peak in-
tensities do not match with synthetic brushite, but
there is a one to one match of the peak positions. Dif-
fraction peaks appear to be quite narrow, indicating
the crystallinity of the deposit. The atomic ratio of
calcium to phosphorus, determined by EDS, revealed
an average composition that is quite close to that of
brushite (Ca: P = 1:1). These two complementary
analyses show the deposit to be brushite.
It is of interest to note the difference in microstruc-
ture of the brushite deposited by this method in com-
parison to that reported earlier.10,11 Figure 3(a) is a
SEM micrograph showing a typical morphology of the
deposit. Throughout the aggregate structure there is
volcano-like structure. However, the volcanoes are
constructed from plates, which are characteristic of
brushite, as can be seen under higher magnification
[Fig. 3(b)].15 We suspect the aggregate structure of the
deposit is due to the presence of hydrogen bubbles
released at the cathode. This conjecture is supported
by the fact that the pores and buildup appear to be
fairly rounded, as if the deposit was formed around
residing gas bubbles. If the bubbles are small enough
they cannot be dislodged by the force of buoyancy and
if conditions are appropriate for the deposition of
brushite, then brushite can deposit around the bubble.
Figure 4 can be used as a model to explain the role of
hydrogen bubbles in brushite deposition.
In this proposed mechanism for the development of
the observed microstructure, it is postulated that the
deposition of brushite takes place due to a pH change
at the surface of the cathode. This is shown in Figure
4(a) where a pH profile is plotted at different times (t
= 0 and t = t). The titanium surface serving as the
cathode reduces the hydrogen ion to its atomic state,
resulting in an increase in the local pH. Hence, the
−2 is greatly increased in comparison
−1. This leads to the increase in con-
−2 until the solubility product of
brushite is exceeded. Thus, a layer of brushite would
coat the cathode [Fig. 4(b)]. However, H atom migra-
tion at the surface can result in H2 molecule formation.
Coalescence of H2 molecules would result in gaseous
macroscopic bubbles. Brushite cannot deposit at sites
occupied by hydrogen bubbles because there is no
mechanism for mass transport through this gas phase.
After a layer of brushite is deposited on the cathode,
because brushite is an insulator, electrons cannot come
to the ceramic solution interface to reduce hydrogen
ions. However, there is a constant current at the cath-
ode and maintenance of a continuous electrode inter-
face would be required. Because H2 is an insulator, ion
migration and diffusion must be along the bubble–
brushite interface. Growth of brushite around the
bubble results in entrapment, until the size of the
bubble is large enough that buoyancy forces can cause
it to dislodge. Until this occurs, brushite will continue
to deposit around the bubble and form a volcano-like
structure [Fig. 4(c)]. When the hydrogen bubble is
Volcano-like buildup of as-deposited brushite.
(a) The micrograph illustrates the gross platelike morphol-
ogy (original magnification ×300). Micrograph (b) shows a
representative structure inside the volcano (original magni-
BRUSHITE COATINGS AND TRANSFORMATION TO HA
large enough, it is dislodged from the surface and so-
lution must flow into this site to occupy the space just
created, enabling fast deposition inside the cone [Fig.
The deposits in the vicinity of the volcano were
found to be rich in potassium. Figure 5(a,b) shows
an SEM micrograph and corresponding EDS potas-
sium map of the volcano region. Potassium (light
spots) is seen to be most intense in regions corre-
sponding to the volcano-like structure, indicating that
it was primarily incorporated after the hydrogen
bubble was dislodged. The deposition of the brushite
inside the hole must have come from a solution that
had a reduced concentration of calcium ions, having
been consumed by brushite precipitation. As the con-
−2 continues to increase, brushite
must precipitate out of solution. The scarcity of cal-
cium ions is compensated by potassium ion incorpo-
ration, making the region inside the volcano structure
Deposits from regions inside the volcano structure
appear to be amorphous [Figs. 3(b), 5(a)]. Walton
documented that coprecipitates can have very small
crystallites, thus making them appear amorphous.16
Redepenning et al. suggested10,11 that the most prob-
able reaction taking place near the cathode is the con-
becoming more basic due to the reduction of the H+
ion at the cathode. Among other ions, Ca+2 is also
present near the cathode; and as the concentration of
−2 ions near the cathode increases, the solubility
product of brushite (Ksp = [Ca2+] ? [(HPO4
2.18 ? 10−7) is exceeded, resulting in the precipitation
of brushite. In the cases of electrolytes A and B, Ca2+
ions are not present in sufficient amounts; thus, K+
ions are used as substitutes. Two potassium ions (size,
1.33 Å) are necessary to replace a single calcium ion
(size, 0.99 Å) in brushite to maintain charge neutrality.
The substitution of two K+ ions for one Ca2+ ion
should induce a large amount of strain in the lattice,
thereby making these regions in the deposits appear
amorphous. The amount of calcium ions present in
these electrolytic solutions (A and B, Table I) is con-
siderably smaller than in solutions C, D, and E. In
deposits made from 7 to 10 g of calcium dihydrogen
phosphate there is enough Ca2+ present to form the
precipitate without the assistance of potassium ions;
therefore, potassium substitution is minimized and
the deposit appears crystalline when viewed in the
SEM. However, even in this composition range, the
regions inside the volcano have high potassium con-
centration, thus making this region look amorphous.
Dissolution studies in all the fluids (Table II) re-
vealed a change in Ca+2 ion concentration relative to
the initial (Figs. 6–8). Solutions I, VI, and VII show an
immediate increase in Ca+2 concentration. In Figure 8
mass change of the deposit with time is also shown for
a modified Hank’s type solution without Ca+2 and
Mg+2 (medium VI). It can be seen that the mass loss
increases with time until about 36 h and then de-
creases, indicating a measurable mass gain. It is noted
that after only 8 h is there a significant amount of
calcium ions in solution. The data also indicate Ca+2
concentration in this fluid decreases after 8 h until
about 18 h, even though the brushite coating is still
undergoing weight loss. This initial weight loss in the
deposit would be due to the release of water of crys-

This conclusion is supported by the fact that EDS
analysis for samples exposed to medium VI for 48 h
show a Ca:P ratio of 1.53:1 as opposed to brushite that
has a ratio of 1:1. This observation is also supported by
X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra obtained for the same
samples. Brushite planes show significant intensity
depletion within 2 h. Figure 9 is a diffraction spectra
sequence that demonstrates reflection from brushite
planes diminishing and that new peaks in the diffrac-
tion spectra extending from 30.5 to 33.5° appear after
about 6 h of dissolution in medium VI. This peak
growth corresponding to HA was found to continue
through 48 h of dissolution. This corresponds to HA.
For clarity and brevity of Figure 9, we did not include
diffraction spectra obtained from samples exposed for
2, 4, and 8 h. However, these spectra show evidence
that our synthetic brushite is transformed to natural
brushite (JCPDS 11-293) before transforming to HA.
Thus, synthetic brushite deposited on the titanium
coupon dissolves in the medium. During its conver-
sion to HA it shows similarity to natural brushite.
There is continuous dissolution and reprecipitation
during the transformation of brushite to HA, not an
abrupt change, suggesting that brushite is a precursor
to HA. Figure 10 shows XRD spectra for the transfor-
mation in medium VII, which is just medium VI with
Ca+2 and Mg+2 added. The same transformation to HA
took place. All the media produced the brushite to HA
transformation. Although all paths lead to HA, the
rate and possibly the transformation mechanisms may
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was per-
formed on samples that were exposed to medium VI.

carbon peaks from the acquired XPS spectra were de-

ratio of the percentage of carbonate carbon to graphitic
carbon and carbonate oxygen to standard oxygen. The
quantitative data obtained are presented in Table III. It
is seen that with increasing time, the ratios of carbon-
ate carbon to graphitic carbon and carbonate oxygen
to standard oxygen increase, thus establishing the
presence of carbonate in the precipitating HA. This
implies that the surface of the precipitating HA has
picked up carbonate, thus becoming more like natural
bone material. The XRD pattern suggested that HA
was the only phase present in the sample after 48 h of
exposure to medium VI. Because the diffraction pat-
tern matched well with JCPDS 9-432 and not with car-
bonate HA (JCPDS 19-272), it is the first few atomic
layers of the precipitated material that has some car-
bonate substitution. This can be expected because me-
depth of XPS is much shallower in comparison to that
of the XRD, information provided by XPS pertains
more to the first few molecular layers on the surface.
This result agrees with work presented by Duchyen et
Bond strength, obtained by the Sebastian Five pull
test,19 between the metal and ceramic revealed low
values for samples made from low concentration cal-
cium dihydrogen phosphate solutions. The highest
values were obtained for deposits made from solution
C. SEM micrographs of the fractured surfaces subse-
quent to the pull test revealed the presence of ceramic
on the sample and nail head, indicating that failure
had taken place in the ceramic layer (Fig. 11). This was
not unexpected because the deposit appears to be very
porous [Fig. 3(a)]. The data obtained from pull tests
performed on deposits made from 7 g of calcium di-
hydrogen phosphate in 100 mL of 1 M KCl solution
were more encouraging. Pull test data are presented in
Tables I and IV. In general, the interfacial bonding
values are not large, although samples deposited for
very small time intervals had higher bond strength
values. Coatings made for short deposition times re-
sulted in a thinner layer and the reduction in the effect

Using a solution of calcium dihydrogen phosphate
in KCl solution, brushite deposition can be induced
electrolytically at an enhanced rate on a conducting
substrate. EDS and XRD spectra confirm the deposit to
be brushite. This deposited layer has regions of high
potassium incorporated in the lattice. When exposed
to various Ca+2 free and Ca+2 containing aqueous me-
dia, the deposit surface shows diminishing XRD peaks
from brushite, but there is a development of diffrac-
tion peaks around 30–33.5° after as little as 6 h of
exposure. The diffraction spectra show transformation
to HA in these media. In some of the media the trans-
formation was complete in 48 h and the transforma-
tion passes through a natural brushite phase very
early. XPS analysis shows that reprecipitation is ac-
companied by carbonate inclusion when the media
contains carbonate, making the surface of precipitated
HA more like natural bone. Atomic adsorption studies
indicated a change in Ca+2 concentration in the expos-
ing media during transformation to HA. EDS analysis
of deposits after 48-h exposure to medium (VI) at
room temperature gave Ca:P ratios of about 1.53:1 as
opposed to a theoretical value of 1.66:1. Thus, it ap-
pears that brushite could be used as a CPC coating
material for implants that could be transformed after
application but before implantation to HA or would
transform in vivo after implantation. It is very cheap to
apply electrolytically and as an added incentive it is a
non line of sight process that is advantageous for coat-
ing porous materials. Brushite deposited from 7 g of
calcium dihydrogen phosphate in 100 mL of 1M KCl
solution demonstrated the best bonding to the sub-
strate. The strength of the deposit closest to the metal
surface showed greater adhesion in comparison to ce-
